description: This is a weak-beam dark field (WBDF) transmission electron microscopy micrograph of a dissociated dislocation in the base austenitic steel, with a scale bar of 50 nm. Annotations in the figure indicate that the diffraction vector g is parallel to the Burgers vector b of the undissociated dislocation (marked as g//b), α refers to the angle between the dislocation line vector and the Burgers vector b, and d denotes the separation distance between two partial dislocations. This micrograph provides direct experimental observation to collect paired (d, α) data points, which are further input into an analytical model to calculate the stacking fault energy (SFE) of austenitic steels. It supports the core research of quantifying the effect of Si and Al alloying on the SFE of austenitic steels by TEM-based dislocation morphology analysis.

This chart presents the dependence of austenite lattice parameter on Si and Al alloying in Fe-based austenitic steels. The x-axis represents relative alloying content in weight percent, while the y-axis shows austenite lattice parameter in nanometer. Three sample groups are included: the base austenitic steel (relative alloying content at 0, lattice parameter ~0.3585 nm), 2.5 wt.% Si alloyed steel (lattice parameter ~0.3587 nm, negligible difference from the base alloy), and 2.5 wt.% Al alloyed steel (lattice parameter ~0.3598 nm, significantly larger than the base alloy). The results demonstrate that Al alloying remarkably increases austenite lattice parameter, while Si alloying has minimal effect on this parameter. These experimental data support accurate lattice parameter-related calculations including stacking fault energy estimation and carbon content prediction for advanced high strength austenitic steel composition design.

This figure consists of two subplots investigating stacking fault energy (SFE) of three austenitic steel alloys: base alloy, 2.5 wt.% Si alloyed alloy, and 2.5 wt.% Al alloyed alloy. Subplot (a) plots partial dislocation separation (unit: nm) as a function of perfect dislocation angle (unit: deg., 0 to 90 deg.). Triangles represent measured data for 2.5 Si alloy, circles for base alloy, squares for 2.5 Al alloy. Solid lines are calculated trends based on average SFE, dashed lines are upper/lower bounds from SFE standard deviation, with measured SFE values 19.9±2.0 mJ/m² for 2.5 Si, 26.3±3.0 mJ/m² for base, 38.3±6.1 mJ/m² for 2.5 Al. Subplot (b) shows SFE dependence on relative alloying content, indicating 2.5 wt.% Al addition increases SFE by 12.0 mJ/m² from base, while 2.5 wt.% Si addition reduces SFE by 6.4 mJ/m². This figure confirms Al alloying increases while Si alloying reduces SFE of austenitic steels, consistent with observed partial dislocation spacing trends.
